description:
You are acting as an in-house Compliance Officer who must prove that a basic “health-check”
    endpoint of an internal application is operational and returns the expected payload.
    The endpoint is exposed locally through a simple file-based URI to avoid any network
    dependencies.

    1. A mock “API” file already exists at
       /home/user/mock_api/health.json
       and contains a single line:
       {"status":"ok"}

    2. Your audit procedure requires that you obtain that payload via a single curl command
       (i.e. one shell command that starts with the word “curl” and, if you need it, any
       redirection operators) and store the result in a log file located at
       /home/user/compliance/api_health.log

    3. The log file MUST contain exactly three lines with the following strict format
       because an automated compliance robot will parse it later:

       • line 1 – an ISO-8601 timestamp in UTC with a literal “Z” at the end, e.g.
                  2023-12-08T14:55:30Z
                  (The seconds field is mandatory; no milliseconds.)
       • line 2 – the HTTP status code returned by curl, which must be literally
                  200
       • line 3 – the raw JSON payload returned by the endpoint, which must be
                  {"status":"ok"}

       No additional characters or whitespace are permitted.

    4. Permissions:
       • The /home/user/compliance directory already exists and is writable by the current
         user.
       • Overwrite the log file if it already exists.

    5. Success criteria that the automated grader will enforce:
       • /home/user/compliance/api_health.log exists.
       • The file has exactly three non-empty lines.
       • First line matches the regex
         ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z$
       • Second line is exactly “200”.
       • Third line is exactly “{"status":"ok"}”.

    6. IMPORTANT: achieve the task using only one curl command (though you may combine it
       with shell redirection operators if needed). You may use other commands afterward
       purely for your own inspection, but the grading logic depends solely on the final
       state described above.

    Example valid content (for illustration only – your timestamp will differ):

       2024-05-01T09:30:12Z
       200
       {"status":"ok"}

    Record the health check now.
truth:
<!--
        Pre-existing filesystem objects created when the container is launched
        (before the agent begins work):
    -->
    <preexisting>
        <directory path="/home/user/mock_api" mode="0755" owner="user" group="user"/>
        <file path="/home/user/mock_api/health.json" mode="0644" owner="user" group="user">
            {"status":"ok"}
        </file>
        <directory path="/home/user/compliance" mode="0755" owner="user" group="user"/>
    </preexisting>

    <!--
        Verification details that the automated test suite will use after the agent finishes:
    -->
    <verification>
        <file path="/home/user/compliance/api_health.log">
            <lines count="3"/>
            <line index="1" regex="^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z$"/>
            <line index="2" equals="200"/>
            <line index="3" equals='{"status":"ok"}'/>
        </file>
    </verification>
name: task_000000_f853d38c
